title: Zero Trust Architecture
description: Never trust, always verify — security model that eliminates implicit trust in any network location.
fullExplanation: Traditional security assumed everything inside the network perimeter was trusted. Zero Trust assumes breach — every request must be authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated regardless of where it originates. Coined by John Kindervag at Forrester in 2010. Google's BeyondCorp (2014) was the first large-scale implementation. NIST SP 800-207 defines the formal ZTA standard.

principles.description: Always authenticate and authorize based on all available data points: identity, location, device health, service/workload, data classification, anomalies.
Limit user access with just-in-time and just-enough-access (JIT/JEA), risk-based adaptive policies, and data protection.
Minimize blast radius, segment access, encrypt end-to-end, use analytics to get visibility and drive threat detection.

pillars.description: Strong identity verification for every user and service account
Verify device health and compliance before granting access
Segment networks, encrypt traffic, eliminate implicit trust
Secure access to applications regardless of location
Protect data at rest, in transit, and in use

vsTraditional.traditional: Castle and moat — trust everything inside perimeter, block everything outside
vsTraditional.zeroTrust: Verify every request — no implicit trust based on network location

keyFacts: Google BeyondCorp: first major ZTA implementation (2014)
NIST SP 800-207: official Zero Trust Architecture standard
ZTNA replaces VPN: identity-based access to specific apps, not entire network
Microsegmentation: isolate workloads to limit lateral movement after breach
Continuous verification: re-authenticate based on risk signals
US Executive Order 14028 (2021): mandates ZTA for federal agencies
